summary: Two towering Psalms frame Israel's story from two angles: Psalm 106 is a corporate confession cataloguing Israel's repeated rebellion from Egypt to Canaan, met each time by God's inexhaustible hesed; Psalm 107 celebrates that same hesed through four archetypal rescue scenes — the desert wanderer, the prisoner, the sick, and the storm-tossed sailor — each crying to God and being delivered, with a closing wisdom summons to consider the steadfast love of the LORD.
psalm_rows_html:
PsalmTypeAuthorSituationKey ThemeNotable VerseNT Connection
106Historical/Penitential Hallel PsalmAnonymous (possibly post-exilic; 1 Chr 16 attributes the opening verses to David)Post-exilic corporate confession; reviews Israel's history of rebellion from the Red Sea through Canaan, closing with a prayer for return from among the nationsIsrael's repeated unfaithfulness and God's covenant-keeping hesed; corporate confession of sin; the pattern of sin-judgment-cry-deliverancev.45 — "For their sake he remembered his covenant and relented according to the abundance of his steadfast love."v.20 quoted by Paul in Romans 1:23 ("they exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images") as the paradigm of all idolatry; the psalm's structure of sin-judgment-cry-mercy prefigures the Gospel pattern of human failure and divine grace in Christ
107Thanksgiving Hymn / Community Song of PraiseAnonymous (likely post-exilic)Likely post-exilic; celebrates God's redemption of scattered Israel through four archetypal deliverance scenes: desert wanderers, prisoners, the sick, sailors in a stormGod's hesed delivering the helpless who cry out; the fourfold refrain "Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love"; divine sovereignty over nature, nations, and individualsv.20 — "He sent out his word and healed them, and delivered them from their destruction."v.20 ("He sent out his word and healed them") read by the early church as a type of Christ the Word sent to heal and save; the four rescue scenes prefigure Jesus's ministry to the lost, sick, captive, and imperiled; v.27 ("at their wits' end") gave English a common idiom via the KJV
